ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302114 covers spring roll as a general category; no specific code exists for deep-fried vegetable spring roll variant. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EJW covers spring rolls as a category. No specific code for deep-fried vegetable-only variant. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for fried vegetable spring roll as a food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 2 pieces of crispy fried vegetable spring roll, approximately 120 g total, as commonly served in Singapore hawker stalls and restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Spring Roll, Fried, Vegetable — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Egg rolls, frozen, prepared (NDB 21052) adapted for vegetable-only filling
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) Foodo; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists fried spring rolls with vegetable filling for Singapore context. USDA SR Legacy NDB 21052 (egg rolls, frozen, prepared) used as secondary cross-reference for macro validation. Values represent a composite estimate for a deep-fried vegetable spring roll using popiah skin, consistent with HPB Foodo data for similar fried snack items. Energy and macros are consistent with published Singapore food composition data for fried vegetable spring rolls (~220 kcal/100g, moderate fat from deep frying).

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of spring rolls and similar fried wheat-wrapper snacks — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for fried dim sum and wheat-based snack foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for crispy fried vegetable spring roll found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 50 based on analogous fried wheat-wrapper snack foods (dim sum, fried wontons, spring rolls) listed in the University of Sydney GI Database, which cluster in the GI 45–55 range. The vegetable filling and wheat-based popiah skin contribute moderate starch with some fibre buffering. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 31.8) / 100 = 15.9, where carbohydrate per 120g serving = 26.5 × 1.2 = 31.8g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg/100g; ~384 mg per 2-piece serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one piece and avoid dipping sauces high in sodium, as a 2-piece serving contributes approximately 384 mg sodium, representing about 17% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~15.9 per 2-piece serving) and refined wheat wrapper
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume fried vegetable spring rolls in moderation, as the refined wheat-based popiah skin and deep-frying process contribute a moderate glycemic load of approximately 15.9 per serving; pairing with non-starchy vegetables and limiting to one piece can help manage postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat from deep frying (~11g/100g) including saturated fat (~2.2g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of deep-fried spring rolls due to elevated total fat content from the frying process; occasional consumption of one piece is acceptable within a diet otherwise low in saturated fat and sodium.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy Fried Vegetable Spring Roll (炸春卷; Popia Goreng Sayur) is a popular deep-fried snack widely available in Singapore hawker centres, food courts, and restaurants. It consists of a thin wheat-based popiah skin (spring roll wrapper) filled with a mixture of fresh vegetables — typically shredded cabbage, julienned carrot, bean sprouts, and glass noodles — which is then rolled tightly and deep-fried in vegetable oil until golden and crispy. The result is a crunchy exterior with a soft, lightly seasoned vegetable filling. The dish is vegetarian and vegan-friendly, and is commonly served as a starter, side dish, or snack, often accompanied by sweet chilli sauce or plum sauce for dipping. Nutritionally, a 2-piece serving (approximately 120g) provides around 264 kcal, 5.4g protein, 13.2g fat, and 31.8g carbohydrate. The sodium content (~384 mg per serving) warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension. The moderate glycemic load (~15.9 per serving) makes it a food that individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume in moderation. The deep-frying process elevates total and saturated fat content, which is relevant for cardiovascular disease management. The dish is a staple of Singaporean Chinese cuisine with roots in Chinese culinary tradition, and is also found across Southeast Asia under various names including Popia Goreng (Malay) and Chun Juan (Mandarin romanization).
common_user_queries: How many calories in a fried spring roll?

confidence_reason: No exact match found in HPB Foodo for this specific product from Viet'Spice restaurant. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo Singapore fried spring roll category data and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy for fried wheat-wrapper snacks. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the adapted source match.
notes: Serving size of 120g (2 pieces) is estimated based on typical Singapore hawker portion for 2 fried spring rolls (~55-65g each). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a vegetable-only filling with no animal products; confirmed zero for vegetable spring roll formulation. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based food containing no animal-derived ingredients; naturally zero. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as this food contains no significant vitamin D sources; naturally zero. GI estimated at 50 (low category, ≤55) based on analogous fried wheat-wrapper snack foods from the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per serving = (50 × 31.8) / 100 = 15.9. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy and HPB Foodo composite data for similar fried vegetable snack items. Original field survey record: food_id GD003479, served by Viet'Spice - Hương vị Việt - Circular Road, price SGD 9.90.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed full nutrient profile from HPB Foodo and USDA SR Legacy adapted composite for fried vegetable spring roll.

Added all mandatory Singaporean multilingual names: Chinese simplified/traditional (炸春卷), Malay (Popia Goreng Sayur), Tamil (வறுத்த காய்கறி ஸ்பிரிங் ரோல்), Hindi (तली हुई सब्जी स्प्रिंग रोल).
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are perfectly consistent — all language-specific alias strings appear verbatim in flat aliases.
Documented zero values for cholesterol_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, vitamin_d_mcg with 'naturally zero' / 'confirmed zero' justifications in notes.
Set ontology match_type values to only allowed values: 'closest_parent' and 'none'.
Confirmed gi_category 'low' is correct for GI=50 (≤55 threshold).
Ensured all per_100g values are numeric; -1 sentinel not used for any field (all estimated from composite source).
